name: Hills by Colorado River
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site along the Colorado River offering agate, jasper, and chalcedony in scenic red rock terrain. Easy to moderate collecting with stunning canyon views.
description:
Hills by Colorado River is a productive BLM rockhounding site in Grand County, Utah, situated in the heart of the Colorado Plateau's red rock country. This public land area provides excellent opportunities for collectors to find quality agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens without permits or fees. The location benefits from the region's rich geological history - the Colorado River has carved through millions of years of layered sedimentary and volcanic deposits, exposing colorful rocks and minerals along its banks and adjacent hillsides.

The terrain around the Colorado River canyon system in this area contains abundant nodules and fragments of banded agate and patterned jasper deposited during ancient silica-rich hydrothermal activity and weathering of Jurassic and Cretaceous formations. Collectors will find material ranging from small thumb-sized pieces to larger specimens suitable for tumbling or display. The river's constant erosion continues to expose new material each season, making repeat visits productive.

This site is best suited for collectors with basic experience navigating rocky terrain, though beginners can succeed by focusing on washes and exposed banks. The combination of scenic beauty, accessible collecting, and reliable gem quality makes this a popular destination for Utah rockhounds. Water access and nearby camping options in the Moab area enhance the appeal for weekend collecting trips.
history: The agate and jasper deposits found here originated during the Cretaceous and early Paleogene periods when silica-rich fluids percolated through sandstone and shale formations, creating nodules and banded layers. The Colorado River's relentless erosion over the past 10 million years has excavated these deposits, transporting and concentrating collectible specimens along banks, in washes, and on adjacent hillsides - a process that continues to expose fresh material today.
tips: Search along the Colorado River banks and within side washes where water erosion concentrates heavier specimens; focus on areas with exposed red and tan-colored hillsides just above the waterline. Look for banded patterns and translucent qualities in agate pieces - specimens with clear banding or color variation are the highest quality finds. Bring a hand lens to examine color patterns and banding before collecting. Early morning collecting in spring (April-May) often yields best results as winter runoff has exposed new material.
terrain_notes: Rolling red rock hills and canyon terrain with elevation around 4,000-4,500 feet. Terrain is rocky and uneven with exposed ledges, loose rock, and scattered boulders typical of Colorado Plateau geology. Walking distance from parking varies from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on chosen area. Footing requires sturdy boots due to sharp sandstone fragments and loose scree. Moderate slopes and some scrambling may be necessary to reach productive areas.
written_directions: From Moab, drive south on US 191 for approximately 12 miles toward the town of Spanish Valley. Look for signed BLM access points and informal parking areas on either side of the highway near mile markers that indicate proximity to the Colorado River. Turn east on unsigned dirt roads or rough tracks leading toward the river canyon - these are typically passable for high-clearance vehicles in dry conditions. Continue 1-3 miles to reach parking areas near productive hillsides and washes. GPS coordinates will provide precise guidance to the specific site.
road_conditions: Rough dirt and rock tracks requiring high clearance. Washboard surface, potential for water crossings during spring runoff. Passenger vehicles not recommended.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and cleared areas along dirt access roads. No developed parking lot. Limited capacity - typically 2-4 vehicles per area. Park well off active roads to allow passage.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation. No power tools or explosives. Hand tools only (hammer, chisel, pry bar). Collect only loose material and surface specimens. Do not damage formations, cliffs, or structures. Leave no trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No power tools or explosives
Hand tools only
Collect loose surface material only
No formation or cliff damage
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Moab, approximately 12 miles north. Full services including gas, restaurants, grocery, camping, lodging.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky ground, exposed ledges, and loose material. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Significant walking over uneven terrain. Some scrambling needed to reach best collecting areas. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or balance issues.

faq.question: What types of agate and jasper can you find at Hills by Colorado River near Moab?
faq.answer: This BLM site is known for banded agate with distinct color patterns, red and tan jasper, and translucent chalcedony specimens. Many pieces show natural banding and color variation ideal for tumbling or display. Quality ranges from thumb-sized fragments to larger 2-3 inch specimens.

faq.question: Do you need a permit or pay a fee to rockhound at Hills by Colorado River in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit or fee is required. This is public BLM land where recreational collecting is allowed free of charge under federal FLPMA regulations. You only need to follow the 25 lb per day personal use limit.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect at this Colorado River rockhounding site?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Spring months (March-April) are particularly productive as winter runoff exposes fresh material along river banks and washes. Summer heat can be intense above 95 degrees, making collecting difficult.

faq.question: How beginner-friendly is rockhounding at Hills by Colorado River for families?
faq.answer: This site is very beginner-friendly. Material is abundant and easy to spot on hillsides and in washes. Children ages 8 and up can participate. No special equipment is needed beyond basic hand tools like a hammer and chisel. The scenic red rock terrain makes it enjoyable even if collecting is slow.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need to bring to Hills by Colorado River?
faq.answer: Bring a hand hammer, cold chisel, pry bar, and collection bag. Wear sturdy hiking boots due to sharp sandstone fragments. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen, water) is essential even in cooler months. A hand lens helps identify quality pieces. A high-clearance or AWD vehicle is needed for the rough access roads.

faq.question: Where exactly should you search for the best agate at this Moab-area rockhounding location?
faq.answer: Focus on washes and side canyons where water erosion has concentrated specimens. Search along exposed tan and red-colored hillsides 10-50 feet above the Colorado River. Check the base of small cliffs and around boulders where material naturally collects. Early morning hours often yield best results.

meta_title: Hills by Colorado River Rockhounding - Utah
meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony on public BLM land near Moab, Utah. No fee, 25 lb daily limit. Scenic Colorado River terrain perfect for rockhounds.
